name: GemsOfINDOLOGY
text:
"Connections Between Vedic and Sumerian Civilizations"

Meru and Sumeru might not be just mythological concepts; they could have coexisted with striking similarities.

Both cultures worshipped sun deities and had similar creation myths involving cosmic battles and detailed genealogies of gods. Linguistic parallels further support these connections.

Despite historical gaps, this analysis offers a glimpse into ancient cross-cultural influences, inviting us to consider how ideas traveled long before modern communication.

#Bookmark this #Thread of #Threads 📚

1/

name: Thibault
text:
My favorite book is The Beginning of Infinity. But it's not an easy read. 

I recently gifted it to a friend along with some notes to help her read it. Here they are in case it's useful for someone else:

- Some chapters are easy to read, others are very complex but all are profound. Skip the difficult chapters on your first read.
- At the end of each chapter, there is a summary. They help with understanding.
- The theme of the book is about the nature of the Universe and the place of humans within it from a rational perspective. In particular, David Deutsch explains that the knowledge we create is like a physical force that can transform everything.
- Many chapters follow the same structure: they discuss ideas commonly accepted as true, only to demonstrate that they are false, and even the exact opposite of the truth.
- The author is a scientist who works at the frontier of physics and philosophy, and who has a deep understanding of biology, history, and many other fields.
- Start with chapters 1, 2, and 3. They are accessible and necessary for the rest of the book as they explain how science (and more generally knowledge) works and what its limits are.
- Chapter 10 is also easy to read. It is a Socratic dialogue about rationality.
- Chapters 8, 11, 5, and 12 are not very easy and take time. The following chapters help to understand evolution (which is the theory of biology): 3, 4, 6, 15, 16, 17.
- Chapters 9 and 18 are optimistic.
- And finally, call me immediately if you have any questions; this is my favorite book!

name: historiakayasthas
text:
🧵 Kayasthas in the Gahadavala realm: An epigraphical survey

Roughly 100 epigraphical records are known from the times of the Gahadavalas, from 11th to 12th centuries CE. Most of these are copper plate land grants to Brahmins. Unfortunately, there is a paucity of large stone inscriptions the likes of which were issued by other dynasties of the times, viz Chandelas, Kalachuris, Paramaras etc. Thus, it is hard to deduce the exact social conditions and the administrative setup of this dynasty. However, the majority of the grants were written by Kayasthas who seem to have monopolised the record keeping in this kingdom. In this thread we will analyse what these records tell us about the contemporary Kayasthas. 

1/12

When Spain finished the Reconquest in 1492, it asked all its Muslims and Jews to either convert, leave or die. Then, it launched the Inquisition to finish off cryptos who were secretly practising Islam. They razed down most of the mosques and reclaimed all their churches.

When Greece attained independence from the Ottoman Empire (and other Eastern European countries), they reclaimed all their churches and razed down all their mosques, and Greeks also had a complete population exchange with Turkey.

When Ireland gained independence in 1920s, the Protestants there were made to flee to the United Kingdom.

We are the only nation in the world which despite being under imperial rule of two minority conquering religions and a bloody partition, who didn't ensure a complete population exchange and struggled for years to get just one temple back. And the West wants to lecture us on minority rights.

name: sushma date
text:
In 1930, a 24 yr old Indian girl completed her PhD in Germany. Her thesis? To prove the superiority of the white race based on the measurements of German and East African skulls, to show that 'white skulls' had better developed right brains ( then known as the seat of reasoning and logic). This was the pet theory of her supervisor Eugen Fischer
 
After meticulously examining 150 skulls, her findings led her to the opposite conclusion, a direct contradiction of her supervisor's theory.  There was no difference between 'white' and 'coloured' skulls - and it must have taken a lot of guts to submit this thesis to him. 

She went on to become India's first female sociologist and anthropologist, led a brilliant academic life with her husband, the equally remarkable Dinkar Karve, and authored many books including my favorite - Yuganta 

Her PhD guide, Eugen Fischer went on to become a Nazi, his theories influenced policies reinforcing racial purity, like forced sterilization of mixed race children and anti Semitic Nuremburg laws

Iru is a fascinating account of one of the pioneering women of India. Do read!

name: Rahul (Proud Bhakt)
text:
Amitabh Bachchan's bungalow stands in Juhu, Mumbai, it's a big bungalow. There are only 2-3 bungalows of big industrialists after AB's bungalow. Along that way (Juhu Tara Road) you'll find a big bungalow. The name of the bungalow is "Nirant”. This bungalow is 3 times bigger than Amitabh Bachchan's bungalow. This bungalow has about 3 acres of lawn and it is very luxurious. You will be surprised at what kind of a luxurious bungalow in a posh area like Juhu in Mumbai is! That bungalow "Nirant" does not belong to any superstar or industrialist. The bungalow belongs to none other than
Teesta Javed Setalvad.
She's just a social worker. From 2004 to 2012 , she received millions of dollars in donations from abroad. 
For what? 
For upliftment of the poor ?
Now there's one more thing .. 
Why are they so much  anti India?  Their forefathers sowed the seed. 
The infamous Hunter Commission was a commission of inquiry that gave a clean chit to General Reginald Dyer, who ordered the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. Harilal Chimanlal Setalvad, who was a member of this commission was the great-grandfather of Teesta Setalvad. It was this Harilal Chimanlal Setalvad's son, i.e. Teesta's grandfather, Motilal Chimanlal Setalvad, who later acquitted General Dyer in the same Jallianwala Bagh case. After independence, Nehru appointed the same Motilal Chimanlal Setalvad, who had acquitted General Dyer, as the Attorney General. One of the many indelible records of Nehru's British allegiance to the British.
The story of seed quality does not end here…… when the same General Dyer was facing trial in the Jallianwala case, it was Diwan Bahadur Kunj Bihari Thapar who declared his steadfast British allegiance by collecting Rs 1.5 lakh on behalf of Gen Dyer and honouring him with an award with a kripan and a turban. Yes, he was actually Karan Thapar's great-grandfather.
The Thapar family is a wealthy family that made their wealth during the First World War, by supplying men and materials to the British for hefty commissions.

When the Thapar family declares their allegiance, don't wonder where the Sikhs' kirpan and turban came from. In fact, it was the management of the Golden Temple that took the initiative. It took place under the auspices of two new rich Singhs, Sujan Singh and  Shobha Singh. There's a reason for that. They were the contractors who were almost entirely in control of the construction work when the capital of British India was shifted from Calcutta to Delhi. Khushwant Singh, the son of Shobha Singh, was a well-known writer and a staunch Indira Gandhi devotee who wrote articles justifying and whitewashing the Emergency. This Khushwant Singh's son, Rahul Singh, still continues the clan’s occupation of anti-Indianism by glorifying people like Teesta Setalvad and Arundhati Roy on NDTV.
Back to the Thapar family... Karan Thapar's father, Pran Nath Thapar, was the Army chief of India who led the war with China in 1962. Pran Nath's predecessor, General KS Thimmaiah, had originally proposed Lt Gen SPP Thorat as his successor. But it was Nehru who sabotaged the proposal and inserted Pran Nath on his own. Another aspect of Nehru's British allegiance.
Not only that, the daughter of Pran Nath's brother, Maya Das Thapar, the real Romila Thapar, who wrote history textbooks in Indian schools. That too was Nehru's nomination.  In school/university history textbooks, you will find nowhere the fact that It was Pran Nath Thapar who led the losing war of 1962. Wouldn't it be surprising if her paternal uncle's name appears as a defeated general in the textbook curated by Romila Thapar!
To some extent, it is these rich families who accepted British bribes and are still driving our sense of history to a certain extent. These same families are the self-proclaimed wholesalers of all progress. They are the ones who bring in false stories and prevent the rise of Indian identity.

name: V Gopalan
text:
Do you spot the lone priest sitting in front of this great temple in the first picture taken in the evening?

Not a soul is there 10 KMs around the temple but his sincerity keeps him in the temple! 

This magnificent temple would have been under the waters of Tungabhadra dam.

Because of the great work of @ASIGoI we see this temple today!

When Tungabhadra dam was built this would have been submerged under the waters.

So, ASI cut this temple into several 1000 bits, numbered each piece carefully & then transported them to a higher level. There they reassembled all the bits and reconstructed this great temple which is full of sculptures!  

To my knowledge this is only a second monument to be reconstructed like this in the world, the other one being Abu Simbel temples at Egypt! 

Unfortunately you will not find this information even in the website of ASI! 

Great guys no?

text:
Explore the Cycle of Creation, Sustenance, and Destruction: The Four Yugas

◆ In Satya Yuga:
Good and evil existed in two separate worlds: Devlok and Asurlok.

◆ In Treta Yuga:
Good and evil lived in the same world, exemplified by Shri Ram and Ravan.

◆ In Dwapara Yuga:
Good and evil were found within the same families: the Pandavas and Kauravas.

◆ In Kali Yuga:
Good and evil have become the same person, highlighting the complex nature of morality in this age.

◆ Each Yuga represents a distinct era of cosmic evolution:

🔸Satya Yuga: The era of purity.
🔸Treta Yuga: The era of righteousness.
🔸Dwapara Yuga: The era of heroism.
🔸Kali Yuga: The era of trials and tribulations.

These cycles shape humanity and the universe, guiding us through the eternal dance of life and time. 🙏

name: Jim O'Shaughnessy
text:
"your brain eats an enormous amount of energy. For example, although it’s capable of capturing the equivalent of eleven megabytes of information per second from your eyes, you can only “upload” about sixty bits per second into the picture you consciously “see.” This is the equivalent of facing the entire population of Paris, France, but actually seeing only eight people. 

To save time and mental energy, your brain does two things. First, it chooses what information to upload. It asks, “What is new here? Has anything changed? If it has, is it important? If not, I’ll save my energy by relying on the norms and patterns I already know and understand.” Second, it makes lightning-fast predictions, filling in missing information based on previous knowledge and experience long before any conscious thought can get involved. By making rapid inferences about what is probably there, your brain tries to make a fairly good guess about what might come next. 

In other words, your brain is not like an objective computer that processes reality as it is. Indeed, the sheer effort of being 100 percent accurate about everything would be an enormous waste of cognitive power. So your brain skips over unimportant details and sticks to those you really need, so that you can make sense of what’s going on in the world, anticipate changes, and react as warranted. 

For example, when you’re crossing a driveway and see a car begin backing toward you, you don’t wait to see what will happen next. You automatically get out of the way. But your brain also primes you for the unexpected. If the car suddenly switches into drive and pulls forward to repark, you will unconsciously compare your prediction about what would happen with what you actually experienced and, if necessary, adjust your future models. Still, since your brain relies so heavily on anticipating what might happen, it has a certain tendency to misinterpret reality as it happens."
~@toddrose

name: Dharmic Living
text:
Honestly speaking - Bhagat Singh, Bose, Gandhi, or anyone else had practically zero effect on the British Empire. 

Do you really think that the British Empire, the largest empire in the world and ruled almost 170 countries, the richest Empire, fought so many wars, brought the Industrial Revolution to the world, was shaken by some guys hanging themselves? 

WW2 drained British Empire's resources and willpower thus Empire weakened.

During WW2, Churchill realized that Britain couldn't defeat the Nazis. So he had approached the US for military support. US President Franklin Roosevelt was aware that whatever conditions he put on Churchill, he would have no other option but to accept. 

So Churchill signed an agreement in 1941, called - The Atlantic charter

The Atlantic Charter had 8 principles. A few of them are decolonization and independence.
The US and other allied forces, like the Soviet Union, forced the British to grant independence to India.

This Atlantic Charter strengthened US-India relations and the US-supported independence for India. 

In 1942- Cripps mission - British proposed India self-rule.

Due to Adolf Hitler and the German nazi British Empire fell.

Strengthened Indian nationalist movement thus granting Indian independence.

Therefore - we got independence not majorly due to freedom fighters but due to WW2, the US and Adolf Hitler, and the nationalist movement post-agreement of the Atlantic Charter and Cripps mission.

So Bhagat Singh or Bose or Gandhi didn't shake the British Empire. Though you can appreciate their efforts on movement but it has no practical impact on the Empire.

name: Rakesh Krishnan Simha
text:
PL Mehta was 10 years old at the time of Partition in 1947. He along with his family of 14 members fled from Pind Dadan Khan in Western Punjab to India. Three refugee trains were to carry the refugees to India. The first train was packed with Sikhs. Since Sikhs were the main targets of Muslim mobs, Mehta's father decided not to travel by that train. They couldn't get on the second train but took the third one.

When the train reached Kamoke station near Lahore it was packed with over 3,500 Hindu refugees. A Muslim mob attacked the train and killed almost all of them, including 11 of Mehta's family.

When the mob climbed onto their wagon, the boy's uncle asked him to lie down on the floor and covered him with his turban. Seconds later the uncle's neck was slashed with a sword. The sword narrowly missed the young Mehta who got drenched in his uncle's blood.

The only ones to escape death were young boys like Mehta or those who were injured. After the mob left, there was still no respite for Mehta because the Baloch soldiers on the platform would shooting anyone who attempted to step out of the train.

Mehta and the few remaining survivors had a stroke of luck when a convoy of Hindu soldiers arrived at the station and escorted them to the hospital in Gujranwala.

However, at the hospital the Muslim doctors started murdering the injured Hindus by injecting them with chemicals. The Hindu non-medical staff at the hospital told the refugees not to allow the doctor to give them injections.

Mehta and the other survivors were taken to Delhi. When the train with 3,500 dead Hindus reached the capital, the people at the station saw the message written by the Pakistanis on the wagons: "Tohfa (Gift) for Hindustan and Gandhi and Nehru."

Both Mohandas Gandhi and Jawaharlal Nehru reached the railway station and when they saw the gruesome sight, all they said was, "No, this can't be true."

But Sardar Patel said, "We will pay back 4 times what they did." After 3 trains of bodies crossed the border, the Pakistanis immediately stopped attacking trains carrying Hindus and Sikhs.

name: GemsOfINDOLOGY
text:
In Rajasthan's Chittorgarh district, historians have uncovered Stone Age rock paintings, cup marks, and various artifacts, shedding light on ancient human existence. These relics, believed to date back between 35,000 and 200,000 years, paint a vivid picture of our early ancestors' lives.

Among the findings, a 2.4kg mortar and sharp-edged stone suggest that early humans used these tools to process wild barley, nuts, and legumes.

#Archaeology

name: Dr. Ricardo Duchesne
text:
One of the biggest customs of the Maori in New Zealand was to conduct raids to capture prisoners to “chop them up and eat them and turn them into excrement”. They would send the excrement to other tribes and tell them how they turned one of their members into excrement.  

“Cannibalism lasted for several hundred years until the 1830s” when whites stopped them. Infanticide for the purpose of eating babies “was also widely practiced” with babies fed to warrior to make them stronger. 

“Mothers often killed their female daughters by smothering them or pushing a finger through the soft tissue of the skull to kill them immediately”. The baby girls were then handed over to warriors to feast upon before battle. 

War was mainly aimed at capturing prisoners to “chop them up and eat them and turn them into excrement”. Academics are covering up this demonic cannibalism but “the amount of evidence is so overwhelming it would be unfair to pretend it didn’t happen.” 

Yet, in New Zealand whites are taught that to talk about Maori cannibalism is racist. The are being encouraged instead to adopt the Maori "Haki" dance as the main cultural symbol of NZ. 

A simple comment I made two days ago in a post about Maori cannibalism has already garnered 270,000 views and 110 comments. https://t.co/W7Id7S8XCp

The evidence about Maori cannibalism is now indisputable, including the practices I quoted above:  
1. https://t.co/GAVyToxZiw

2. There is whole book about this: Horrid Practice: The Myth and Reality of Traditional Maori Cannibalism (2008), by New Zealand historian Paul Moon. Wiki has an article about it: https://t.co/zdY6yfeJbJ

name: ISRO Spaceflight
text:
Yesterday, ISRO revealed the first ever sub-surface temperature readings from the Moon's South Polar region!
 
They used the ChaSTE instrument on the Vikram Lander to drill a probe into the Lunar surface and take temp. readings at various depths.🌡

Results show that while the temp. at the surface is ~50°C 🔥, it quickly goes down to -10°C ❄️ only 8 cm below the surface!

This observation increases the hopes of finding water ice below the surface near the Moon's South Pole! 🧊

Image credit: @VikranthJonna
#Chandrayaan3 #ISRO

You may not know that the derivatives of the position vector (x) with respect to time have interesting names:

Velocity (v) = dx/dt
Acceleration (a) = d²x/dt²
Jerk (j) = d³x/dt³
Snap (s) = d⁴x/dt⁴ 
Crackle (c) = d⁵x/dt⁵
Pop (p) = d⁶x/dt⁶

name: Rajiv Mehta
text:
Those who can spare some time must read this to know the behavioural context : And it was written more than 100 years ago and still equally relevant 

The Unbearable Lightness of Being                                                                                                                                                                             Joe Wiggins                                                                                                                                                                                                     

A friend of mine recently asked if I had read a book called Psychology of the Stock Market. Given the subject matter, I was quite surprised that I hadn’t come across it. Even more surprising is that it was first published over 100 years ago. So, how do George Charles Selden’s thoughts on investor and market behaviour from 1912 compare with today? Has much changed?

Let’s look at some highlights:

---

“The market is always a contest between investors and speculators.”

Selden argues that there are two types of market participants. Investors – who are focused on the fundamental attributes of a security or asset, and speculators – who are concerned only with the direction of the price.

---

“The speculator cares nothing about interest return…He would as soon buy at the top of a big rise as at any other time.”

The idea that large swathes of investors have no real concern about the underlying value of a security is similar to something I wrote about valuation and price in 2022. He just got there a little earlier than me.

---

“However firm may be his bearish convictions, his nervous system eventually gives out under this continual pounding, and he covers everything…with a sigh of relief that his losses are no greater.”

Selden writes of a short seller who - despite retaining a stridently negative opinion – capitulates because of the pain of being the wrong side of a trade. Evoking not only loss aversion, but the emotional strain of being wrong.

---

“It is hard for the average man to oppose what appears to be the general drift of public opinion. In the stock market it is perhaps harder than elsewhere.”

We are inescapably drawn towards the behaviour of the crowd. Taking and maintaining contrary views can be exacting and exhausting.  

---

“the average man is an optimist regarding his own enterprises and a pessimist regarding those of others…he comes habitually to expect everyone else will be wrong, but is, as a rule, confident that his own analysis of the situation will prove correct.”

This description of overconfidence from Selden still resonates today. The odds and evidence are against everyone else successfully picking stocks, timing the market or making economic predictions – but, of course, I can do it.

---

“If you are long or short the market you are not an unprejudiced judge, and you will be greatly tempted to put such an interpretation upon current events as will coincide with your preconceived opinion.”

Confirmation bias remains as strong now as it was back in 1912.

---

“When the market looks weakest, when the news is at the worst, when bearish prognostications are most general, is the time to buy, as every school boy knows; but…it is almost impossible for him to get up the courage to plunge in and buy.”

Here Selden describes the difference between our behaviour in a hot and cold state. In our cool, calculating moments, it is easy to plan what we will do when markets are in turmoil; yet when that moment arrives our emotions will take hold with fear and anxiety overwhelming us. Selden was unknowingly advocating systematising future investment decision.

---

“It is a sort of automatic assumption of the human mind that present conditions will continue”

Extrapolation remains one of the most damaging investor behaviours.

----

“Some events cannot be discounted, even by the supposed omniscience of the great banking interests.”

name: Abishek K V - SEBI RA
text:
If you think mid 20s to 30s is easy, no its not. While you have been busy figuring your S**t in early 20s, by the time you hit 27-28; you realize parents are growing older; your favourite uncles/aunts are growing older. 

You see subtle changes in their behavior. Could be slower movements, holding railing while using staircase or take naps more frequently. All of it hits a person differently. 

The hard absolute irrevocable truth about the finality of life is something that takes a lot of time to come to terms with.

ADX22 swing strategy:

Exit 1/2 when ADX22 crosses above 35
Exit remaining 1/2 when price closes below Supertrend (10,2)

stock = accumulated reputation

flow = slope of reputation.

There are 3 primary drivers of results in life:

1) Your luck (randomness).
2) Your strategy (choices).
3) Your actions (habits).

Only 2 of the 3 are under your control.

But if you master those 2, you can improve the odds that luck will work for you rather than against you.

name: Shane Parrish
text:
Anyone can do something once. 

One time success is largely based on what you know (and luck). 

Repeated success, however, is less about what you know and more about how you respond to what you don't know.

name: James Clear
text:
Concentration produces wealth. 

Diversification protects wealth.

name: Prof. Feynman
text:
The FEYNMAN technique of learning:

STEP 1 - Pick and study a topic

STEP 2 - Explain the topic to someone, like a child, who is unfamiliar with the topic

STEP 3 - Identify any gaps in your understanding 

STEP 4 - Review and Simplify!

name: Naval
text: Leverage is a force multiplier for your judgement.

name: Naval
text: Judgement requires experience, but can be built faster by learning foundational skills.

name: Naval
text: When specific knowledge is taught, it’s through apprenticeships, not schools.

name: Naval
text: Building specific knowledge will feel like play to you but will look like work to others.

name: Naval
text: Specific knowledge is knowledge that you cannot be trained for. If society can train you, it can train someone else, and replace you.

name: Naval
text: Learn to sell. Learn to build. If you can do both, you will be unstoppable.

name: Naval
text: Play iterated games. All the returns in life, whether in wealth, relationships, or knowledge, come from compound interest.

name: Naval
text: Pick an industry where you can play long term games with long term people.

name: Naval
text: You will get rich by giving society what it wants but does not yet know how to get. At scale.

name: Naval
text: The strongest intellectual foundation is built upon science, math, and philosophy, as they are the search for universal truths.

name: Naval
text: Set and enforce an aspirational personal hourly rate. If fixing a problem will save less than your hourly rate, ignore it. If outsourcing a task will cost less than your hourly rate, outsource it.

name: Naval
text: Study microeconomics, game theory, psychology, persuasion, ethics, mathematics, and computers.

name: yoyopaps
text: @naval @naval Can you explain more deeply "Leverage is a force multiplier for your judgement."

name: Naval
text: @peterwyoo Judgement is making decisions. Leverage magnifies the consequences of those decisions. Invest $100 of your money or $10,000 of someone else’s. Work on a project for 1 year or work with a team of 10 on that project for a year. Advise a friend or advise many via Twitter.

text:
Crowds are complex. The more they mourn your departure, the more they welcome new policies.
